title: Blame the Bot: Anthropomorphism and Anger in Customer–Chatbot Interactions
hypothesis_4omini: When customers enter a chatbot-led service interaction in an angry emotional state, chatbot anthropomorphism negatively affects customer satisfaction, overall firm evaluation, and subsequent purchase intentions, but this effect does not occur for customers in nonangry emotional states.

title: A Meta-Analysis of When and How Advertising Creativity Works
hypothesis_4omini: Advertising creativity has robust positive effects, which are stronger for high-involvement products and marginally stronger for unfamiliar brands, with originality leading to affect transfer and appropriateness leading to signaling. Further research is needed to connect advertising creativity with sales and its effects in digital contexts.

title: A Theories-in-Use Approach to Building Marketing Theory
hypothesis_4omini: The article discusses the development of marketing knowledge through the theories-in-use approach, emphasizing the creation of novel marketing theories and the co-creation of knowledge with practitioners, while providing guidance on selecting suitable research questions and developing theoretical propositions.

title: The Role of Advertising in High-Tech Medical Procedures: Evidence from Robotic Surgeries
hypothesis_4omini: Hospital advertising positively influences patients' choice of robotic surgery over conventional surgeries, particularly among Medicaid patients, despite higher costs and no long-term health benefits.

title: Advertising Effectiveness for Multiple Retailer-Brands in a Multimedia and Multichannel Environment
hypothesis_4omini: The study finds that email and sometimes catalogs from a focal retailer-brand negatively influence other retailer-brands in the category, while paid search influences only the focal retailer-brand; however, competitor catalogs positively influence focal retailer-brand sales among omnichannel customers, and a sizeable group of customers who shop across multiple retailer-brands and both purchase channels are the most responsive to multimedia advertising.

title: Analyzing the Cultural Contradictions of Authenticity: Theoretical and Managerial Insights from the Market Logic of Conscious Capitalism
hypothesis_4omini: This research demonstrates that the cultural contradictions of authenticity are influenced by the tensions between conscious capitalism and elitist critiques, affecting how consumers and marketers understand and manage authenticity in various market contexts.

title: Artificial Intelligence Coaches for Sales Agents: Caveats and Solutions
hypothesis_4omini: The incremental benefit of AI coaches over human managers is heterogeneous across sales agents, with middle-ranked agents showing the largest performance improvement, while bottom- and top-ranked agents experience limited gains due to information overload and aversion to AI, respectively. Redesigning the AI coach can alleviate challenges faced by bottom-ranked agents, and the combination of AI and human coaches outperforms either alone.

title: Bringing the Doctor to the Patients: Cardiology Outreach to Rural Areas
hypothesis_4omini: The allocation of cardiologists' time between home and outreach locations is significantly influenced by travel distance, potential competition, and patient demand, and a subsidy program can effectively restore rural access to cardiology services after a reduction in cardiologist supply.

title: Dynamic Governance Matching in Solution Development
hypothesis_4omini: The study identifies the dynamic role of governance matching in solution development, revealing that solutions evolve through phases of experimentation, integration, and evolution, where governance mechanisms serve different functions and provide contingent benefits, influencing the success or failure of solution efforts.

title: Business-to-Business E-Negotiations and Influence Tactics
hypothesis_4omini: The use of compliance or internalization-based influence tactics as textual cues in B2B e-negotiations enhances buyer attention, which in turn increases the likelihood of contract award, while the concurrent use of both tactics may degrade buyer attention and disadvantage the salesperson in closing the contract.

title: Can Viagra Advertising Make More Babies? Direct-to-Consumer Advertising on Public Health Outcomes
hypothesis_4omini: Increased erectile dysfunction drug television advertising leads to a higher birth rate, with a 1% increase in advertising contributing to an increase of 0.04% to 0.08% in total births.

title: Capital Market Returns to New Product Development Success: Informational Effects on Product Market Advertising
hypothesis_4omini: The capital market reacts with abnormal stock returns to new product development success events, which influences subsequent marketing decisions, particularly that higher unpredicted abnormal approval returns lead to larger postapproval advertising budgets and more effective postlaunch advertising, especially for detailing spending.

title: Caring for the Commons: Using Psychological Ownership to Enhance Stewardship Behavior for Public Goods
hypothesis_4omini: Increasing consumers' psychological ownership enhances stewardship behavior for public goods by increasing perceived responsibility, which leads to active care behaviors; this relationship is weakened by cues that diffuse responsibility.

title: Charting the Path to Purchase Using Topic Models
hypothesis_4omini: The proposed integrated model connects latent topics in consumers' search phrases to their website visits and purchase decisions, providing better predictive accuracy and improving marketing efficiency compared to other models.

title: Choosing the Light Meal: Real-Time Aggregation of Calorie Information Reduces Meal Calories
hypothesis_4omini: Real-time aggregation of calorie content using a traffic light label reduces calories ordered in meals, as consumers revise high-calorie orders more frequently and report greater satisfaction and intentions to return to restaurants using this feedback method.

title: Collaborative Market Driving: How Peer Firms Can Develop Markets Through Collective Action
hypothesis_4omini: The article conceptualizes "collaborative market driving" as a collective strategy where peer firms cooperate to develop markets, which increases their overall competitiveness, and identifies the triggers for collective action and the conversion of resources into market-driving power.

title: Commentary: A Strategic Perspective on Capturing Marketing Information to Fuel Growth: Challenges and Future Research
hypothesis_4omini: The root of the growth problem facing managers is broader than data capture, requiring a significant refocusing of the academic research agenda to connect data potential to realized growth outcomes.

title: Commentary: Artificial Intelligence: The Marketer’s Dilemma
hypothesis_4omini: Marketers face significant challenges when utilizing AI, as they are increasingly dependent on technology and technologists, which blurs the line between their control and the capabilities of AI, leading to a dilemma in their marketing strategies.

title: Commentary: Toward Formalizing Social Influence Structures in Business-to-Business Customer Journeys
hypothesis_4omini: The study highlights the unique challenges in studying social customer journeys for B2B firms and emphasizes the need for research related to structural dynamics and key performance indicators in this context.

title: Commentary: Trajectories and Twists: Perspectives on Marketing Agility from Emerging Markets
hypothesis_4omini: The commentary aims to assess the robustness and durability of marketing agility in extreme conditions of uncertainty and change, particularly in the context of digital products in emerging markets.

title: Communication in the Gig Economy: Buying and Selling in Online Freelance Marketplaces
hypothesis_4omini: Buyers and freelancers in online freelance marketplaces can achieve success by balancing communication uncertainty rather than merely reducing it, with specific strategies regarding task information, personal information, and affective intensity influencing bid attraction and success.

title: Commentary: Managing Human Experience as a Core Marketing Capability
hypothesis_4omini: The shift from "solid" material goods to "liquid" experiential goods is a broader concept that deserves more weight in considering how to deepen perceived ownership in the eye of the consumer, and experiences are not only replacing material goods but also being used to enhance them. Marketers should build internal capabilities to manage the shift to experiences in the face of digitization.

title: Conceptual Contributions in Marketing Scholarship: Patterns, Mechanisms, and Rebalancing Options
hypothesis_4omini: The study finds that conceptual contributions in marketing scholarship have increasingly favored "envisioning" and "explicating" over "relating" and "debating," with a prevalent thought style shaping these contributions, and suggests rebalancing options to enhance the latter contributions while maintaining current strengths.

title: Conducting Research in Marketing with Quasi-Experiments
hypothesis_4omini: The article aims to broaden the understanding of quasi-experimental methods in marketing by identifying causal relationships between actions and marketing outcomes, emphasizing the importance of empirical strategies and behavioral mechanisms in reinforcing causality.

title: Consumer Self-Control and the Biological Sciences: Implications for Marketing Stakeholders
hypothesis_4omini: The appreciation of biological underpinnings of human behavior can alter the beliefs and actions of marketing stakeholders, and narrowing the gap between scientific and lay perspectives is crucial for improving welfare.

title: Consumers and Artificial Intelligence: An Experiential Perspective
hypothesis_4omini: This research aims to bridge the perspectives of the benefits of AI technology for consumers and the social and individual challenges that arise from its deployment, identifying four types of consumer experiences with AI: data capture, classification, delegation, and social, while discussing policy and managerial avenues to enhance consumer value.

title: Cueing Morality: The Effect of High-Pitched Music on Healthy Choice
hypothesis_4omini: Listening to higher-pitched music increases consumers' likelihood to choose healthy options, choose lower-calorie foods, and engage in health-boosting activities by raising the salience of morality thoughts.

title: Customer Experience Journeys: Loyalty Loops Versus Involvement Spirals
hypothesis_4omini: The study proposes that customer experience journeys can be categorized into two models—loyalty loops and involvement spirals—each influencing customer behavior differently, with loyalty loops fostering customer loyalty through predictable experiences and involvement spirals enhancing customer involvement through unpredictable experiences.

title: Customer Satisfaction and Its Impact on the Future Costs of Selling
hypothesis_4omini: Higher customer satisfaction can lower future cost of selling (COS), but the degree of this benefit depends on the firm's strategy and operating environment. The negative effect of customer satisfaction on future COS is weaker for firms with higher capital intensity and financial leverage, and stronger for more diversified firms and those in industries with higher growth and labor intensity. These effects may vary across the components of COS, cost of persuasion and convenience.

title: Complaint Publicization in Social Media
hypothesis_4omini: The phenomenon of complaint publicization in social media occurs when firm responses to complaints increase the potential public exposure of those complaints, negatively impacting perceived quality and firm value, especially for firms targeted by retail investors.

title: Do Animated Line Graphs Increase Risk Inferences?
hypothesis_4omini: Animated display of time-varying data enhances risk judgments by increasing the visual salience of transitions, leading to inflated risk judgments and consequential investment decisions.

title: Do Promotions Make Consumers More Generous? The Impact of Price Promotions on Consumers’ Donation Behavior
hypothesis_4omini: Price promotions increase consumers' perceived resources, which in turn increase consumers' donation behavior, but this effect is attenuated when consumers focus on the amount spent, feel they have overspent, or when savings cannot be realized immediately, and is stronger when solicitation occurs immediately after the promotion.

title: Does Bad Medical News Reduce Preferences for Generic Drugs?
hypothesis_4omini: Receiving bad medical news prompts patients to favor brand name drugs over generic drugs as a means to safeguard their health, with an observed increase of 8% in the likelihood of choosing brand name alternatives.

title: Effects of Payment on User Engagement in Online Courses
hypothesis_4omini: Paying for a certificate in online courses increases user engagement through the certificate effect and the sunk-cost effect, with the certificate effect lasting until the participant reaches the required grade for the certificate and the sunk-cost effect being transient.

title: Effects of Contract Ambiguity in Interorganizational Governance
hypothesis_4omini: Contract ambiguity of franchisor obligations increases interest-based conflict solving approaches, leading to greater cooperation and joint problem solving, and reduces franchisee-initiated litigation, with effects amplified by higher levels of franchisor training programs but mitigated by the presence of a franchisee association.

title: Emotional Calibration and Salesperson Performance
hypothesis_4omini: The emotional intelligence--sales performance link can be better understood by considering a salesperson's emotional self-efficacy (ESE), and sales performance suffers when salespeople are either overconfident or underconfident in their emotional skills, with optimal performance occurring when they are emotionally calibrated; performance gains associated with emotional calibration are attenuated under stress and occur due to positive avoidance emotions that improve customer rapport, particularly among salespeople with longer job tenures.

title: Emotional Expressions and Brand Status
hypothesis_4omini: There is a negative relationship between brand emotionality and brand status, and brands can increase perceived brand status by reducing emotionality in communications, moderated by brand status context and product type.

title: Examining Why and When Market Share Drives Firm Profit
hypothesis_4omini: The positive effect of market share on firm profit is primarily explained by market power and quality signaling, with little support for operating efficiency, and the value of market share varies predictably between firms and across industries.

title: Face Forward: How Employees’ Digital Presence on Service Websites Affects Customer Perceptions of Website and Employee Service Quality
hypothesis_4omini: The digital presence of service employees on the firm website increases customer perceptions of website service quality and employee service quality, which indirectly enhances customer loyalty and financial performance, with effects amplified by employee accessibility and customer orientation. The beneficial spillover effect is specific to service employees and does not extend to product quality evaluations.

title: Faculty Research Incentives and Business School Health: A New Perspective from and for Marketing
hypothesis_4omini: The faculty research incentive system misaligns with the actual contributions to business school health, as research quantity positively impacts research health but not other dimensions, while research rigor has a stronger positive effect on research health, and practical importance does not contribute to research health but positively affects teaching health.

title: Fear of Detection and Efficacy of Prevention: Using Construal Level to Encourage Health Behaviors
hypothesis_4omini: Illness detection evokes fear, which undermines engagement in detection behaviors, while illness prevention is driven by self-efficacy perceptions, and considering prevention at high levels of thought increases persuasion; framing a detection action as serving illness-prevention goals increases engagement with detection tools.

title: Free Shipping Promotions and Product Returns
hypothesis_4omini: Free shipping promotions encourage customers to make riskier purchases, leading to more product returns, and while they increase net sales volume, they also result in higher product returns and lost shipping revenue, making these promotions unprofitable.

title: From Waste to Taste: How “Ugly” Labels Can Increase Purchase of Unattractive Produce
hypothesis_4omini: "Ugly" labeling increases the purchase likelihood of unattractive produce by correcting consumers' biased expectations regarding key attributes, particularly tastiness, and is most effective when associated with moderate price discounts.

title: Genetic Data: Potential Uses and Misuses in Marketing
hypothesis_4omini: The article proposes a framework that incorporates genetic influences into consumer behavior theory, suggesting potential marketing uses of genetic data while evaluating ethical challenges related to autonomy, privacy, misinformation, and discrimination.

title: GMO Labeling Policy and Consumer Choice
hypothesis_4omini: Consumer demand for GM foods is influenced by the labeling regime adopted by policymakers, with both absence-focused and presence-focused labeling reducing market share, and the design of labels affecting consumer responses, particularly among those with neutral attitudes toward GMOs.

title: Happiness Begets Money: Emotion and Engagement in Live Streaming
hypothesis_4omini: A happier broadcaster makes the audience happier and begets intensified viewer activities, particularly tips, and these effects are pronounced only after a live stream has been active for a while, manifesting in streams by more experienced, popular broadcasters.

title: How (and When) the Presence of Food Decreases Enjoyment of Customer Experiences
hypothesis_4omini: The presence of food decreases the enjoyment of concurrent experiences, particularly positive ones, by prompting mental imagery of consuming the food, which reduces engagement with the target experience; this effect is moderated by the valence of the target experience and can also occur through instructed mental imagery in the absence of food.

title: How Do Fast-Fashion Copycats Affect the Popularity of Premium Brands? Evidence from Social Media
hypothesis_4omini: Fast-fashion copycats can harm high-end brands through a cannibalization effect and help them through a market expansion effect, influenced by both static and dynamic mechanisms.

title: How Industries Use Direct-to-Public Persuasion in Policy Conflicts: Asymmetries in Public Voting Responses
hypothesis_4omini: Industries use direct-to-public persuasion strategies to gain public support, with effectiveness varying based on argument type and public suspicion, leading to asymmetric responses in voting behavior favoring activist groups.

title: How Main Street Drives Wall Street: Customer (Dis)satisfaction, Short Sellers, and Abnormal Returns
hypothesis_4omini: Customer satisfaction positively influences abnormal stock returns, while customer dissatisfaction negatively influences abnormal stock returns, with short seller activity mediating this relationship. Additionally, customer satisfaction and dissatisfaction have a more significant impact on firms with low capital intensity and lower competitive intensity.

title: Improving Cancer Outreach Effectiveness Through Targeting and Economic Assessments: Insights from a Randomized Field Experiment
hypothesis_4omini: Targeted outreach marketing increases screening completion rates and improves the return on outreach programs, demonstrating significant variations in treatment effects based on patient demographics and other factors.

title: Improvised Marketing Interventions in Social Media
hypothesis_4omini: The effect of improvised marketing interventions (IMIs) on online virality and firm value is enhanced by humor and timeliness or unanticipation, as evidenced by five multimethod studies.

title: In Mobile We Trust: The Effects of Mobile Versus Nonmobile Reviews on Consumer Purchase Intentions
hypothesis_4omini: Knowing a review was posted from a mobile device can lead consumers to have higher purchase intentions due to the assumption that mobile reviews are more physically effortful to craft, which is equated with greater credibility of the review.

title: Putting the Person Back in Person-Brands: Understanding and Managing the Two-Bodied Brand
hypothesis_4omini: The interdependent relationship between person and brand can be managed through consistency and balance, with factors like mortality, hubris, unpredictability, and social embeddedness influencing brand value.

title: Incentives Increase Relative Positivity of Review Content and Enjoyment of Review Writing
hypothesis_4omini: Incentives increase the relative positivity of review content and enhance the enjoyment of review writing, with the effect being stronger when incentives are strongly associated with review writing and when they transfer positive affect.

title: Increasing Team Performance by Sharing Success
hypothesis_4omini: The maximum contract, where team output is determined by the largest individual sales output, leads to greater overall team effort compared to the average contract when team members have heterogeneous sales abilities, as the increase in effort from stronger team members exceeds the decrease in effort from weaker team members.

title: Influencer Mix Strategies in Livestream Commerce: Impact on Product Sales
hypothesis_4omini: The effectiveness of influencer marketing strategies in livestream commerce is influenced by the mix of big and small influencers, where a negative interaction effect exists between them, and the sales generated by a big influencer are adversely affected by prior promotions from small influencers, while small influencers are more effective at increasing conversion rates.

title: Informational Challenges in Omnichannel Marketing: Remedies and Future Research
hypothesis_4omini: This article investigates the challenges of data access and integration, marketing attribution, and consumer privacy protection in omnichannel marketing, and argues that machine learning and blockchain technologies offer promising solutions while presenting new challenges and opportunities for firms.

title: Innovation Imprinting: Why Some Firms Beat the Post-IPO Innovation Slump
hypothesis_4omini: Firms that engage in innovation imprinting before going public attract investors whose risk preferences support breakthrough innovation, enabling them to maintain or exceed pre-IPO levels of breakthrough innovations after going public.

title: Onboarding Salespeople: Socialization Approaches
hypothesis_4omini: The decentralized onboarding program, which incorporates both individualized and institutionalized socialization tactics, leads to higher sales performance in salespeople compared to the centralized program, with performance benefits being amplified for those with managers having a narrower span of control and being appreciable for those transitioning from another job but negligible for those transitioning from school.

title: JM: Promoting Catalysis in Marketing Scholarship
hypothesis_4omini: Journals can serve as catalysts in marketing scholarship, facilitating knowledge transfer and engagement among stakeholders, thereby advancing the marketing discipline and its broader community.

title: Large-Scale Cross-Category Analysis of Consumer Review Content on Sales Conversion Leveraging Deep Learning
hypothesis_4omini: The study finds that aesthetics and price content significantly increase sales conversion across almost all product categories, with the impact of review content being higher when the average rating is higher, ratings variance is lower, the market is more competitive or immature, or brand information is not accessible. Additionally, reordering reviews based on content can have the same effect as a 1.6% price cut for boosting conversion.

title: Leveraging Creativity in Charity Marketing: The Impact of Engaging in Creative Activities on Subsequent Donation Behavior
hypothesis_4omini: Engaging potential donors in creative activities positively influences their donation behaviors, enhancing felt autonomy and inducing a positive affective state that leads to higher donation behaviors, which are mediated by perceptions of donation impact and a desire for mood maintenance. The effects are context independent and do not occur with noncreative activities.

title: Machiavellianism in Alliance Partnerships
hypothesis_4omini: Machiavellianism in an alliance negatively influences collaborative learning, positively influences learning anxiety and use of power, and these factors mediate its impact on performance, with partners' collaborative history moderating these relationships.

title: Managing Laggards: The Importance of a Deep Sales Bench
hypothesis_4omini: The bench program, which involves the threat of replacement for poor performers, has a significant and sustained positive impact on the performance of laggards, especially when compared to a program with a similar threat but no replacement, indicating that the vividness of a threat enhances its deterrent value.

title: Marketing Ideas: How to Write Research Articles that Readers Understand and Cite
hypothesis_4omini: Scholars write unclearly due to the curse of knowledge, which leads to the use of more abstract, technical, and passive writing, making articles harder to understand and less likely to be cited.

title: Marketplace Donations: The Role of Moral Identity Discrepancy and Gender
hypothesis_4omini: Women primed with moral traits give about 20% more than men, and this effect is influenced by the moral identity discrepancy, which is larger for women who have historically given less.

title: Meaning of Manual Labor Impedes Consumer Adoption of Autonomous Products
hypothesis_4omini: The meaning of manual labor acts as a barrier to the adoption of autonomous products, with consumers who derive meaning from manual tasks evaluating these products less favorably and adopting them less frequently; however, emphasizing alternative sources of meaning can increase adoption.

title: Mickey D’s Has More Street Cred Than McDonald’s: Consumer Brand Nickname Use Signals Information Authenticity
hypothesis_4omini: Brand nickname use enhances perceived information authenticity and boosts purchase willingness and information sharing, with the effect being attenuated in firm-generated content.

title: Multichannel Strategies for Managing the Profitability of Business-to-Business Customers
hypothesis_4omini: A customer's search and purchasing in the seller's online channels positively interacts with both salesperson contact and customer-specific discounts to drive the seller's customer-level sales and profit return on these investments.

title: Nonprofit Versus For-Profit Health Care Competition: How Service Mix Makes Nonprofit Hospitals More Profitable
hypothesis_4omini: Nonprofit hospitals achieve higher output, prices, and profits than for-profit hospitals by expanding their service mix with high-priced premium specialty medical services, and competition increases the differences between nonprofit and for-profit hospitals in PSMS breadth, output, and prices.

title: On the Path to Decolonizing Health Care Services: The Role of Marketing
hypothesis_4omini: The deployment of marketing techniques and technologies can improve health outcomes for First Nations people and support the decolonization of health services.

title: Path to Purpose? How Online Customer Journeys Differ for Hedonic Versus Utilitarian Purchases
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers' information channel choices vary significantly with the hedonic or utilitarian characteristics of purchases, as evidenced by the differing usage patterns of social media, on-site product pages, and third-party reviews during the customer journey.

title: Pleasant Ambient Scents: A Meta-Analysis of Customer Responses and Situational Contingencies
hypothesis_4omini: Exposure to pleasant ambient scents produces a substantial increase in customer responses (3%--15%), with effects depending on situational contingencies such as congruency, aroma structure, scent familiarity, service exchange, gender proportion, and offering type.

title: Political Identity and Financial Risk Taking: Insights from Social Dominance Orientation
hypothesis_4omini: Conservatives' financial risk taking increases with self-efficacy due to social dominance orientation, while liberals' financial risk taking remains invariant to self-efficacy; the mediating effect of focus on upside potential is present among conservatives but not liberals.

title: Price Promotions Cause Impatience
hypothesis_4omini: Incidental exposure to price promotions triggers reward seeking, which causes greater impatience in unrelated domains, as evidenced by increased willingness to pay to avoid waiting, shorter actual wait times, greater propensity to break rules to save time, and greater discounting in intertemporal choices, with effects being more pronounced for individuals with higher reward sensitivity and mediated by reward seeking.

title: Context Information Can Increase Revenue in Online Display Advertising Auctions: Evidence from a Policy Change
hypothesis_4omini: Providing context information in online display advertising auctions increases average revenue per impression, particularly for reputable publishers and high-quality sites, while sites with thin markets do not experience a rise in prices.

title: Providers Versus Platforms: Marketing Communications in the Sharing Economy
hypothesis_4omini: Provider-focused marketing communications in peer-to-peer business models lead to higher consumer perceptions of provider independence, increased willingness to pay, and greater likelihood of purchase and app download, as consumers adopt an empathy lens.

title: Commercial Success Through Commercials? Advertising and Pay-TV Operators
hypothesis_4omini: The study finds that consumers in the U.S. pay television service market are sensitive to advertising, particularly that of satellite operators, with ad elasticities of about .05--.06 for satellite operators compared to .02 for cable operators, and that asymmetric cost efficiencies in television advertising have benefited satellite operators more than cable operators, highlighting the interaction between advertising efficiencies and market competition.

title: Relative Effectiveness of Print and Digital Advertising: A Memory Perspective
hypothesis_4omini: Print ads are more effective than digital ads in terms of encoding and engagement, leading to better memory retention, particularly due to superior encoding during initial exposure.

title: Revenue Generation Through Influencer Marketing
hypothesis_4omini: Targeting low-followership influencers generates higher immediate revenue compared to high-followership influencers, with engagement mediating the negative relationship between influencer followership levels and return on investment.

title: Ritualistic Consumption Decreases Loneliness by Increasing Meaning
hypothesis_4omini: Ritualistic behavior involving consumer products reduces loneliness by increasing meaning in life, with the effect mediated by perceived product meaningfulness, and this effect is diminished when meaningfulness is derived incidentally.

title: Selectively Emotional: How Smartphone Use Changes User-Generated Content
hypothesis_4omini: Smartphone use leads to the generation of briefer user-generated content that emphasizes emotional aspects over specific details compared to PC use, driven by the nature of smartphone constraints rather than user self-selection or other factors.

title: Shedding Light on the Dark Side of Firm Lobbying: A Customer Perspective
hypothesis_4omini: Lobbying negatively affects customer satisfaction, and this negative relationship is mediated by losses to customer satisfaction, with several customer-focused moderators attenuating this effect.

title: Should Your Brand Pick a Side? How Market Share Determines the Impact of Corporate Political Advocacy
hypothesis_4omini: The net effect of corporate political advocacy (CPA) at the market level is determined by a sorting process that benefits small-share brands and hurts large-share brands, with authenticity being a necessary condition for small-share brands to benefit.

title: Social Media, Influencers, and Adoption of an Eco-Friendly Product: Field Experiment Evidence from Rural China
hypothesis_4omini: The use of low-cost social media tools can effectively promote the adoption of a new eco-friendly pesticide in rural China by alleviating farmer uncertainty, although influencer credibility is necessary to resolve issues related to supplier credibility and product authenticity during the trial stage.

title: Speed Up, Size Down: How Animated Movement Speed in Product Videos Influences Size Assessment and Product Evaluation
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers estimate the size of an immobile product to be smaller when it is animated to move faster in videos, influenced by learned size-speed associations, with various factors affecting this relationship, including perceived movement similarity, accessibility of positive size-speed associations, consumer knowledge, and explicit product size information.

title: Task-Dependent Algorithm Aversion
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers are averse to relying on algorithms for subjective tasks, but increasing the perceived objectivity of these tasks and the algorithms' affective human-likeness can enhance trust and usage of algorithms.

title: The Commercial Consequences of Collective Layoffs: Close the Plant, Lose the Brand?
hypothesis_4omini: Following collective layoff announcements, layoff firms experience adverse changes in sales, advertising elasticity, and price elasticity, with a significant decrease in sales by 8.7%, advertising elasticity by 9.8%, and an increase in price elasticity by 19.2%, particularly influenced by announcement characteristics.

title: The Concept of Authenticity: What It Means to Consumers
hypothesis_4omini: The authors reconceptualize authenticity as a holistic consumer assessment determined by six component judgments (accuracy, connectedness, integrity, legitimacy, originality, and proficiency), which vary according to the consumption context, and demonstrate that authenticity has consumer-relevant downstream consequences while being conceptually distinct from consumer attitudes.

title: The Control–Effort Trade-Off in Participative Pricing: How Easing Pricing Decisions Enhances Purchase Outcomes
hypothesis_4omini: This research proposes that firms' delegation of pricing decisions to consumers creates a control-effort trade-off, where greater pricing control is favored by consumers but the effort involved in deciding what to pay can deter purchase intentions; strategies that enhance feelings of control without increasing perceived effort can improve pricing outcomes.

title: The Differential Effect of Local–Global Identity Among Males and Females: The Case of Price Sensitivity
hypothesis_4omini: Gender identity and local-global identity affect consumer price sensitivity, with identity incongruence depleting cognitive resources and inducing affective processing, leading to a sacrifice mindset and lower price sensitivity.

title: The Double-Edged Effects of E-Commerce Cart Retargeting: Does Retargeting Too Early Backfire?
hypothesis_4omini: Early e-commerce cart retargeting ads delivered within 30 minutes to one hour after cart abandonment have a negative impact on purchase rates compared to not delivering them, while late ads delivered one to three days after abandonment have a positive impact on purchases.

title: The Effect of Links and Excerpts on Internet News Consumption
hypothesis_4omini: Linking increases celebrity news consumption, especially among consumers who browse the least, and benefits both the linking and linked sites. Exposure to a link increases the likelihood of visiting the linked site by 0.14%.

title: The Impact of Advertising Creative Strategy on Advertising Elasticity
hypothesis_4omini: The advertising creative strategy (ACS) significantly impacts advertising elasticity, with experiential content having the greatest effect, and the combination of function and form producing synergies that enhance returns. Additionally, focus, variation, and the use of templates are found to increase advertising elasticity.

title: The Impact of Platform Protection Insurance on Buyers and Sellers in the Sharing Economy: A Natural Experiment
hypothesis_4omini: Platform-level buyer protection insurance (PPI) significantly increases buyer spending and seller revenue in the sharing economy, with buyer-side responses including increased product orders and variety-seeking behavior, and seller-side responses including enhanced customer retention and acquisition.

title: The Impact of Soda Taxes: Pass-Through, Tax Avoidance, and Nutritional Effects
hypothesis_4omini: The tax on sweetened beverages leads to a significant price increase and a decrease in demand, with tax avoidance through cross-shopping limiting revenue generation and nutritional improvement.

title: The Journal of Marketing Research Today: Spanning the Domains of Marketing Scholarship
hypothesis_4omini: The study finds substantial diversity in domains, topics, methods, and sources of data among articles published in the Journal of Marketing Research, with an increasing convergence across articles in the behavioral, quantitative, and strategy domains, reflecting shared substantive topics and common use of methods and sources of data.

title: The Platformization of Brands
hypothesis_4omini: The platformization of brands through flagship platforms enhances consumer loyalty and control over brand offerings, while the consequences of crowdsourcing and crowdsending affect consumer-platform relationships, leading to a typology of relationship states and propositions for offline-born product brands to thrive.

title: The Rise of New Technologies in Marketing: A Framework and Outlook
hypothesis_4omini: New technologies in marketing lead to innovations in marketing techniques, tools, and strategies, providing firms that adopt them with a competitive advantage over those that do not.

title: The Role of Gender in Pay-What-You-Want Contexts
hypothesis_4omini: Men typically pay less than women in pay-what-you-want settings due to gender differences in agentic versus communal orientation, which shape payment motives and ultimately affect payment behavior. Sellers can increase how much men pay by enhancing the salience of social payment motives without altering how much women pay.

title: The Sales Impact of Using Handheld Scanners: Evidence from the Field
hypothesis_4omini: Handheld scanner use increases sales through unplanned, healthier, and impulsive purchases, but may decrease sales for consumers without a budget; the influence of scanners on sales is mediated by cognitive and affective mechanisms.

title: The Secret Ingredient Is Me: Customization Prompts Self-Image-Consistent Product Perceptions
hypothesis_4omini: Customization influences product perceptions based on individuals' self-image, leading to self-image-consistent product perceptions, which vary across product categories and attributes.

title: They’re Just Not That into You: How to Leverage Existing Consumer–Brand Relationships Through Social Psychological Distance
hypothesis_4omini: Marketers can effectively manage existing brand relationships by focusing on the psychological distance between consumers and brands, matching close brands with concrete language and distant brands with abstract language to create a mindset-congruency effect that leads to more favorable evaluations and behaviors.

title: Too Time-Crunched to Seek Variety: The Influence of Parenting Motivation on Consumer Variety Seeking
hypothesis_4omini: Parenting motivation affects consumer variety seeking by inducing feelings of time crunch, which leads to less variety seeking, with the effect diminished by time-saving support, perceived time availability, and lack of brand loyalty.

title: Traveling with Companions: The Social Customer Journey
hypothesis_4omini: The social customer journey integrates the role of social companions and their social distance from the decision maker, influencing the purchase decision and highlighting new research opportunities in marketing.

title: Turning Complaining Customers into Loyal Customers: Moderators of the Complaint Handling–Customer Loyalty Relationship
hypothesis_4omini: The effectiveness of complaint handling in promoting customer loyalty depends on various factors, including economic conditions, industry competition, product type, and customer characteristics, with stronger relationships observed in faster-growing economies and for luxury products, while weaker relationships are noted for high reliability expectations and among male customers.

title: Uniting the Tribes: Using Text for Marketing Insight
hypothesis_4omini: Textual analysis can generate marketing insights by reflecting the qualities of the text producer and impacting the audience, thereby uniting interdisciplinary marketing approaches.

title: Up, Up, and Away: Upgrading as a Response to Dissimilar Brand Users
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers with strong self-brand connections prefer upgrading to more exclusive products as a response to dissimilar brand users, driven by feelings of self-threat and a desire for higher status, with moderators affecting this upgrading behavior.

title: Variable Compensation and Salesperson Health
hypothesis_4omini: An increasing variable compensation share increases salespeople's stress, resulting in emotional exhaustion and more sick days, particularly for those with lower personal ability and fewer social resources, which in turn reduces the positive effects of this incentive on sales performance.

title: Investigating the Effects of Excise Taxes, Public Usage Restrictions, and Antismoking Ads Across Cigarette Brands
hypothesis_4omini: The study suggests that while cigarette excise taxes decrease smoking rates, they also lead to a market share shift towards stronger brands, and that smoke-free restrictions cause a shift away from these brands, with minimal effects from antismoking advertising on brand choice and consumption.

title: When Algorithms Fail: Consumers’ Responses to Brand Harm Crises Caused by Algorithm Errors
hypothesis_4omini: Following a brand harm crisis, consumers respond less negatively if the error was caused by an algorithm (vs. a human), and this relationship is mediated by consumers' lower mind perception of agency of the algorithm, which lowers their perceptions of the algorithm's responsibility for the harm. Four moderators of this relationship are hypothesized: whether the algorithm is anthropomorphized, whether it involves machine learning, whether the task is subjective (vs. objective), and whether it is interactive (vs. noninteractive).

title: When and How Slow Motion Makes Products More Luxurious
hypothesis_4omini: The speed of video ads influences consumers' perceptions of luxuriousness, with slow motion increasing perceived luxuriousness and subsequent desire for the product, mediated by feelings of immersion and hedonic value expectations, and moderated by video quality and consumer predisposition to experience immersion.

title: When Salespeople Manage Customer Relationships: Multidimensional Incentives and Private Information
hypothesis_4omini: The sales performance-moral hazard trade-off is influenced by multidimensional performance incentives, where acquisition incentives induce moral hazard leading to adverse customer selection, while maintenance incentives moderate this effect, highlighting the importance of multidimensional incentives for customer relationship management.

title: Prosocial Goal Pursuit in Crowdfunding: Evidence from Kickstarter
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers' prosocial motives play a significant role in reward-based crowdfunding, influencing their contribution patterns by leading to faster funding collection right before meeting funding goals, especially for projects that evoke prosocial motivation and when the creator is a single individual.

title: Where Do You Want to Go for Dinner? A Preference Expression Asymmetry in Joint Consumption
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers take on "requestor" or "responder" roles in joint consumption decisions, leading to a preference expression asymmetry where requestors desire preference expressions while responders do not, resulting in preference and social friction costs for responders.

title: First-Price Auctions in Online Display Advertising
hypothesis_4omini: The transition from second-price to first-price auctions in online display advertising is driven by the shift from waterfalling to header bidding, which improves publisher revenue; however, this shift may lead to lower revenue for some exchanges due to increased competition, despite all exchanges adopting first-price auctions in equilibrium.

title: Soda versus Cereal and Sugar versus Fat: Drivers of Healthful Food Intake and the Impact of Diabetes Diagnosis
hypothesis_4omini: Households with higher education and nutrition interest consume fewer calories, sugar, and total carbohydrates, while those with higher self-control consume more; consumption of sugar and carbohydrates decreases significantly following a diabetes diagnosis, but fat and sodium intake increases; younger and higher-income households, particularly those with a female diabetes patient, make healthier changes.

title: Is a Picture Worth a Thousand Words? An Empirical Study of Image Content and Social Media Engagement
hypothesis_4omini: Social media posts with pictures induce higher engagement than those without, with high-quality images consistently leading to higher engagement, while the effect of colorfulness varies by product category and the presence of human faces and image-text fit enhances engagement on Twitter but not on Instagram.

title: Brand Name Types and Consumer Demand: Evidence from China’s Automobile Market
hypothesis_4omini: The study finds that Chinese consumers prefer vehicle models with semantic brand names, leading to 7.64% more sales compared to alphanumeric names, while showing the least preference for phonosemantic names, resulting in 4.92% lower sales than alphanumeric names; domestic firms benefit from semantic names, while foreign firms gain from foreign-sounding names, with entry-level products performing better with semantic names and high-end products excelling with foreign-sounding names.

title: Capturing Marketing Information to Fuel Growth
hypothesis_4omini: The study suggests that marketers can leverage rich data from various sources to develop effective growth strategies, but face challenges due to overreliance on readily available data, which hinders the connection between marketing data growth and firm growth.

title: Commentary: Beyond Data: The Mindsets and Disciplines Needed to Fuel Growth
hypothesis_4omini: Organizations must shift their mindset towards customer-centricity and develop critical disciplines to drive successful business transformation beyond data-driven marketing techniques.

title: Complaint De-Escalation Strategies on Social Media
hypothesis_4omini: Increasing active listening and empathy in firm responses to social media complaints mitigates negative arousal and evokes gratitude among customers in high-arousal states, even if the actual failure is not yet recovered.

title: Effect of Alliance Network Asymmetry on Firm Performance and Risk
hypothesis_4omini: The absolute levels of alliance network asymmetry have curvilinear effects on alliance performance and performance uncertainty, specifically that direct tie asymmetry has an inverted U-shaped effect on abnormal returns and a U-shaped effect on risk, while indirect tie asymmetry has a U-shaped effect on risk, with innovation quality and preexisting ties flattening these effects.

title: Fields of Gold: Scraping Web Data for Marketing Insights
hypothesis_4omini: The authors propose a novel methodological framework that enhances the validity of web data collection by addressing technical and legal/ethical concerns across three stages: selecting data sources, designing data collection, and extracting data.

title: Making the World a Better Place: How Crowdfunding Increases Consumer Demand for Social-Good Products
hypothesis_4omini: Crowdfunding increases consumer demand for social-good products by activating an interdependent mindset, which is contingent on high collective efficacy and is eliminated when the product is already on the market.

title: Influencer Marketing Effectiveness
hypothesis_4omini: Influencer originality, follower size, and sponsor salience enhance influencer marketing effectiveness, while posts announcing new product launches diminish it, and influencer activity, follower–brand fit, and post positivity exert inverted U-shaped moderating effects on effectiveness.

title: Leaving Something for the Imagination: The Effect of Visual Concealment on Preferences
hypothesis_4omini: Visual concealment tactics can enhance aesthetic product evaluations by triggering curiosity, which leads to positive affect that inflates preferences, but this effect only occurs when initial inferences about the product's appeal are positive or emotionally congruent.

title: Machine Learning for Creativity: Using Similarity Networks to Design Better Crowdfunding Projects
hypothesis_4omini: The prior level of success of similar crowdfunding projects strongly predicts a new project's funding performance, and funding performance increases with a balance between novelty and imitation, with optimal funding goals being close to the funds raised by prior similar projects.

title: Organizational Herding in Advertising Spending Disclosures: Evidence and Mechanisms
hypothesis_4omini: Firms learn from peers' decisions to reduce uncertainty in advertising disclosure, leading to herding effects among peer firms in the same strategic group, with a preference for resolving disclosure uncertainty from similar peers rather than benchmark leaders.

title: P2V-MAP: Mapping Market Structures for Large Retail Assortments
hypothesis_4omini: The proposed P2V-MAP approach effectively analyzes market structures by deriving latent product attributes from shopping basket data, facilitating analyses of cross-category product complementarity and within-category substitution, while being automated, scalable, and easy to interpret, as validated through simulation and comparison with econometric methods.

title: Platform Exploitation: When Service Agents Defect with Customers from Online Service Platforms
hypothesis_4omini: The phenomenon of platform exploitation occurs when high-quality, long-tenured service agents enhance platform usage but also lead customers to defect off the platform, with this behavior increasing with greater customer-agent interaction frequency, higher service prices, and more repetitive services.

title: Psychological Ownership of (Borrowed) Money
hypothesis_4omini: The degree to which consumers feel psychological ownership of borrowed money predicts their willingness to borrow money for discretionary purchases, and this psychological ownership is distinct from other individual factors and varies by debt type. Additionally, framing debt using language lower in psychological ownership can reduce consumers' propensity to borrow.

title: Pretty Healthy Food: How and When Aesthetics Enhance Perceived Healthiness
hypothesis_4omini: Prettier food is perceived as healthier because classical aesthetic features make it appear more natural, and this effect is mediated by perceived naturalness and moderated by belief in a "natural = healthy" connection.

title: When and Why Saying “Thank You” Is Better Than Saying “Sorry” in Redressing Service Failures: The Role of Self-Esteem
hypothesis_4omini: The shift from emphasizing service providers' fault (apology) to spotlighting consumers' merits (appreciation) increases consumers' self-esteem and post-recovery satisfaction, with appreciation being a more effective strategy than apology in redressing service failures.

title: A Meta-Analysis of Brand Extension Success: The Effects of Parent Brand Equity and Extension Fit
hypothesis_4omini: Parent brand equity and extension fit positively influence extension success, with different dimensions of these drivers having varying effects, such as usage fit having the weakest effect and brand familiarity having a lower interaction effect with extension fit than other dimensions of parent brand equity.

title: “Choozing” the Best Spelling: Consumer Response to Unconventionally Spelled Brand Names
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers are less likely to support unfamiliar brands with unconventionally spelled names compared to those with conventional spellings, due to perceptions of insincerity stemming from the belief that unconventional spelling is a persuasion attempt; however, this effect can be mitigated if the motive for the unconventional spelling is perceived as sincere, and such names may be desirable when consumers seek a memorable experience.

title: Better Marketing for a Better World
hypothesis_4omini: The marketing discipline must reflect on its social relevance in addressing critical societal issues such as war, poverty, and environmental contamination, as marketers can either contribute to or help resolve these problems.

title: Customer Satisfaction and Firm Profits in Monopolies: A Study of Utilities
hypothesis_4omini: Customer satisfaction positively predicts future profitability of utility firms by reducing operating costs, even when it does not affect future revenues. Higher satisfaction leads to lower distribution, customer service, and sales and general administration expenses, generating efficiency-enhancing benefits and fostering greater trust and cooperation from customers.

title: Educational Campaigns for Product Labels: Evidence from On-Shelf Nutritional Labeling
hypothesis_4omini: Educational campaigns can increase consumer awareness and understanding of nutritional labels, leading to a preference for higher star-rated foods; however, the effect is modest and diminishes after the campaign's conclusion.

title: Finding Goldilocks Influencers: How Follower Count Drives Social Media Engagement
hypothesis_4omini: There is an inverted U-shaped relationship between influencers' follower count and engagement with sponsored content, where engagement first increases and then decreases as follower count rises, and this relationship is moderated by content customization and brand familiarity.

title: Generating Content Increases Enjoyment by Immersing Consumers and Accelerating Perceived Time
hypothesis_4omini: Generating content during an experience increases feelings of immersion and accelerates perceived time, which enhances enjoyment of the experience, with consumers benefiting from content creation regardless of whether it is incentivized or organic.

title: Households Under Economic Change: How Micro- and Macroeconomic Conditions Shape Grocery Shopping Behavior
hypothesis_4omini: Micro and macroeconomic conditions significantly influence grocery shopping behavior, with microeconomic changes primarily affecting overall purchase volume and macroeconomic changes causing structural shifts in shopping basket allocation and spending behavior.

title: How Marketing Perks Influence Word of Mouth
hypothesis_4omini: Low-contractuality marketing perks are more effective than high-contractuality perks at fostering word of mouth, particularly by conveying a relational signal that motivates consumers to share word of mouth, with conditions under which this effect may attenuate or reverse.

title: Innovation Potential, Insider Sales, and IPO Performance: How Firms Can Mitigate the Negative Effect of Insider Selling
hypothesis_4omini: A firm's innovation potential is positively associated with the initial value of the IPO and first-day IPO returns, while being negatively associated with the extent of insider selling at the time of the IPO. Different metrics of innovation potential have varying impacts on these relationships.

title: Marketing, Through the Eyes of the Stigmatized
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers with potentially stigmatizing attributes decode marketing communications as audience cues, which influence their social devaluation inferences and subsequently affect their beneficial and detrimental consumption behaviors. Anticipated empowerment may increase beneficial consumption among these consumers.

title: Modeling Dynamic Heterogeneity Using Gaussian Processes
hypothesis_4omini: The authors propose a novel framework for capturing dynamic heterogeneity using Gaussian processes, which allows for flexible variations around population-level trends and provides precise individual-level insights regarding parameter dynamics, with robust evidence of dynamic heterogeneity found in preference dynamics and online reviews.

title: The Dark Side of Mobile App Adoption: Examining the Impact on Customers’ Multichannel Purchase
hypothesis_4omini: The adoption of a hotel group's mobile app leads to a significant and negative change in customers' multichannel spending, influenced by factors such as the use of the app for mobile check-in versus searching, and the potential adoption of competitor apps.

title: The Economic and Social Impacts of Migration on Brand Expenditure: Evidence from Rural India
hypothesis_4omini: Economic remittances result in greater brand expenditure, particularly for poorer households, and the effect of migration on brand expenditures is more positive for households in more populous villages with greater access to mobile phones and lower viewership of television media.

title: The Strength of Weak-Tie Consensus Language
hypothesis_4omini: Weak ties are more influential than strong ties in the interpretation and persuasiveness of consensus language, as weak ties evoke perceptions of a larger and more diverse group, signaling greater validity for the issue at hand.

title: The Value of Rapid Delivery in Omnichannel Retailing
hypothesis_4omini: Faster delivery in the online channel positively affects sales in omnichannel retailing, with an average increase of 1.45% in online store sales per business-day reduction in delivery time, and a positive spillover effect to offline stores, driven by customer learning and brand presence. Customers with less online experience respond more in the short run, while experienced customers respond more in the long run.

title: Identifying Market Structure: A Deep Network Representation Learning of Social Engagement
hypothesis_4omini: The proposed deep network representation learning framework effectively captures latent relationships among brands and social media users, allowing firms to identify dynamic product-market boundaries and leverage insights for competitive advantage.
